patterns.intent: Show a single chat surface that carries both user-injected sigil commands (bypassing the model and writing directly to state) and threshold-gated unsolicited agent pushes.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: a single channel that splits on a sigil prefix and rate-limits agent-initiated pushes.
Production variant: a stream loop with backpressure, a cancel-token that interrupts mid-push, and a per-window quota that survives reconnect.
examples.code:
SIGIL                = "!"
UNSOLICITED_WINDOW   = 600     # seconds
MAX_INFLIGHT_PUSHES  = 2       # backpressure ceiling

state = {
    "memory":        load_memory(),
    "last_push_ts":  load_persisted("last_push_ts", default=0),
    "inflight":      0,
    "cancel":        new_cancel_token(),
}

function on_user_message(text):
    if text == "!stop":
        state.cancel.fire()                       # interrupt any active push
        return ack("cancelled")
    if text.startswith(SIGIL):
        verb, args = parse_sigil(text[1:])
        apply_to_memory(state.memory, verb, args) # bypasses the model
        return ack(verb)
    state.cancel = new_cancel_token()             # reset per user turn
    stream = agent.reply_stream(text, state, cancel=state.cancel)
    for chunk in stream:
        if state.cancel.fired(): break
        emit(chunk, marker="tentative")           # partial-result render
    emit_marker("final")

function on_agent_push(candidate):
    if state.inflight >= MAX_INFLIGHT_PUSHES:     # backpressure
        log("dropped: inflight cap", candidate); return
    if now() - state.last_push_ts < UNSOLICITED_WINDOW:
        return
    state.inflight += 1
    try:
        push_to_user(candidate.message)
        state.last_push_ts = now()
        persist("last_push_ts", state.last_push_ts)   # survives reconnect
    finally:
        state.inflight -= 1
# Why this works: the quota is persisted so reconnects do not reset the
# rate-limit, the cancel token interrupts both user replies and pushes,
# and the inflight ceiling stops a slow consumer from queueing pushes.
# Related: see `salience-triggered-output`, `stop-cancel`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: bidirectional-impulse-channel composed with `salience-triggered-output` and `audit`, so every sigil and every unsolicited push lands in the audit log.

patterns.intent: Show how to stream citation events interleaved with text deltas so the UI can render source links in place as content appears.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: extract citation markers from model output into typed events alongside text deltas, accumulating a final citation map.
Iterate streamed message chunks and route Citation annotations on text content blocks into separate UI events.
examples.verified_notes: Confirmed against langchain_core/messages/content.py at master: Citation TypedDict has type='citation', id, url, title, start_index, end_index, cited_text, extras fields. TextContentBlock has type='text', text, annotations: NotRequired[list[Annotation]] where Annotation = Citation | NonStandardAnnotation. init_chat_model is the documented LangChain v0.3 entrypoint and model.stream() yields AIMessageChunk whose content is a list of content blocks.

examples.intent: Consume streamText's fullStream and split text-delta events from source events so the UI renders citations as they arrive.
examples.verified_notes: Confirmed against https://ai-sdk.dev/docs/ai-sdk-core/generating-text: streamText, fullStream, 'text-delta' and 'source' event types all documented, with sourceType==='url' and source fields id, url, title shown verbatim. The google_search tool via google.tools.googleSearch({}) is documented on the same page with @ai-sdk/google. 'finish' event in fullStream switch is also documented.

patterns.intent: Show the preprocessing step that interleaves source and target streams with a fixed offset, turning streaming speech into a single-decoder autoregressive problem with no learned read/write policy.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: tokenise source and target onto a shared time axis, offset target by a fixed delay, then interleave for a vanilla next-token loss.
examples.code:
PAD = "<pad>"

function build_training_example(source_audio, target_text, delay_steps):
    src = tokenise_aligned(source_audio)            # one token per time step
    tgt = tokenise_aligned(target_text, src)        # same time axis, possibly sparse

    # Shift target right by `delay_steps`; pad the prefix so the decoder cannot
    # see future source at any step. The offset IS the read/write policy.
    tgt_shifted = [PAD] * delay_steps + tgt
    tgt_shifted = tgt_shifted[:len(src)]

    interleaved = []
    for t in 0..len(src)-1:
        interleaved.append((src[t], tgt_shifted[t]))   # pair per timestep

    return interleaved   # train standard decoder-only LM to predict next pair

function infer(stream_source, delay_steps):
    buffer = []
    for src_tok in stream_source:
        buffer.append(src_tok)
        out = model.step(src_tok)                      # next-token over pair
        if len(buffer) > delay_steps:
            emit(out.target_token)                     # read off at the offset
# No policy net; latency is a hyperparameter, not a learned decision.

examples.intent: Production variant: a real-time DSM decode loop with backpressure on the source, a cancel-token for barge-in, and consistency markers separating tentative emissions from finalised ones.
Composition variant: DSM composed with `streaming-typed-events`, so the offset-aligned token stream surfaces as typed `partial_transcript`/`final_transcript` events on one transport.
examples.code:
DELAY_STEPS = 16

function dsm_to_typed_events(stream_source):
    buffer = []
    last_event_id = 0
    yield sse(id=++last_event_id, event="turn_start", data={})
    for src_tok in stream_source:
        buffer.append(src_tok)
        out = model.step(src_tok)
        if len(buffer) <= DELAY_STEPS:
            continue                              # inside the offset window
        # Partial transcript: tentative, the same offset structure
        # that drives DSM also drives the typed event vocabulary.
        yield sse(id=++last_event_id,
                  event="partial_transcript",
                  data={"text": out.target_token,
                        "step": len(buffer)})
        if out.is_segment_boundary:
            yield sse(id=++last_event_id,
                      event="final_transcript",
                      data={"text": out.segment_text,
                            "step": len(buffer)})
    yield sse(id=++last_event_id, event="done", data={})
# Why this works: DSM keeps the decoder-only architecture and the
# fixed-offset read policy, while streaming-typed-events gives the
# client a typed vocabulary it can route on. The offset is what
# turns each model step into a partial-vs-final event, with no
# learned policy layered on top.
# Related: see `streaming-typed-events`, `multilingual-voice-agent`.

patterns.intent: Show a minimal proxy schema (energy, fatigue, environment) the human updates and the agent reads on every prompt assembly, with response shape conditional on the proxy.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: a tiny persistent proxy file the human writes and the agent reads to shape its reply.
Production variant: streaming reply that re-reads the proxy mid-stream, cancels and reshapes if the human updates state, and marks tokens emitted before the change as tentative.
Composition variant: embodied-proxy-handoff composed with `interruptible-agent-execution`, so a proxy change becomes a first-class interrupt that the agent loop drains and replans against.

patterns.intent: Show how to compute a small feature set from message timing and tone, map it to a mode, and condition response shape on that mode.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: extract timing + tone features, classify mode, and feed mode into the prompt assembly.
examples.code:
ANCHOR_HOUR = 7    # the user's known morning anchor

function extract_features(msg, prev_ts):
    now = current_time()
    return {
        "hour":         now.hour,
        "hours_to_anchor": min_distance(now.hour, ANCHOR_HOUR),
        "gap_seconds":  (now - prev_ts).seconds if prev_ts else infinity,
        "len":          len(msg.text),
        "punct_density": punctuation_count(msg.text) / max(1, len(msg.text)),
        "single_emoji": is_solo_emoji(msg.text),
    }

function classify_mode(f):
    if f.hours_to_anchor <= 1 and f.len < 40:        return "just-woke"
    if f.hour >= 22 or (f.len < 30 and f.single_emoji): return "winding-down"
    if f.gap_seconds < 30 and f.len > 200:           return "focused"
    if f.gap_seconds > 1800 and f.len < 20:          return "distracted"
    return "present"

function respond(msg, prev_ts):
    mode = classify_mode(extract_features(msg, prev_ts))
    shape = {
        "just-woke":    "one anchor surface, no questions",
        "winding-down": "short, no homework",
        "focused":      "deeper engagement, no hedging",
        "distracted":   "hold; acknowledge only",
        "present":      "normal",
    }[mode]
    telemetry.emit({"mode": mode, "shape": shape})
    return agent.reply(msg, response_shape=shape)

examples.intent: Production variant: classify mode per turn, stream the reply with tentative/final markers, and abort + reclassify if the inferred mode changes mid-stream.
examples.code:
ANCHOR_HOUR = 7

state = { "cancel": new_cancel_token(), "mode": "present" }

function classify_mode(features):
    if features.hours_to_anchor <= 1 and features.len < 40: return "just-woke"
    if features.hour >= 22 or (features.len < 30 and features.single_emoji):
        return "winding-down"
    if features.gap_seconds < 30 and features.len > 200: return "focused"
    if features.gap_seconds > 1800 and features.len < 20: return "distracted"
    return "present"

function respond_stream(msg, prev_ts):
    feats          = extract_features(msg, prev_ts)
    state.mode     = classify_mode(feats)
    state.cancel   = new_cancel_token()
    shape          = shape_for(state.mode)
    stream         = agent.reply_stream(msg, shape=shape, cancel=state.cancel)
    written        = 0
    for chunk in stream:
        if state.cancel.fired():
            emit_marker("tentative", chars=written)
            emit_marker("reshape", new_mode=state.mode)
            return respond_stream(msg, prev_ts)            # reclassify
        emit(chunk, marker="tentative")
        written += len(chunk)
        # Recheck mode periodically — a long pause mid-stream from the
        # user can flip them from focused to distracted.
        if written % 200 == 0:
            new_mode = classify_mode(extract_features(msg, prev_ts))
            if new_mode != state.mode:
                state.mode = new_mode
                state.cancel.fire()
    emit_marker("final")

function shape_for(mode):
    return {
        "just-woke":    "one anchor surface, no questions",
        "winding-down": "short, no homework",
        "focused":      "deeper engagement, no hedging",
        "distracted":   "hold; acknowledge only",
        "present":      "normal",
    }[mode]
# Why this works: classifying once per turn misses mid-stream drift;
# the loop recomputes the mode while writing and uses a cancel token
# plus tentative/final markers to recover the surface when the user
# slips from one state to another.
# Related: see `embodied-proxy-handoff`, `now-anchoring`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: liminal-state-detection composed with `audit`, so every classified mode plus its derived shape is logged for later tuning of the thresholds.
examples.code:
ANCHOR_HOUR = 7

function extract_features(msg, prev_ts):
    n = current_time()
    return {
        "hour":            n.hour,
        "hours_to_anchor": min_distance(n.hour, ANCHOR_HOUR),
        "gap_seconds":     (n - prev_ts).seconds if prev_ts else infinity,
        "len":             len(msg.text),
        "punct_density":   punctuation_count(msg.text) / max(1, len(msg.text)),
        "single_emoji":    is_solo_emoji(msg.text),
    }

function classify_mode(f):
    if f.hours_to_anchor <= 1 and f.len < 40: return "just-woke"
    if f.hour >= 22 or (f.len < 30 and f.single_emoji): return "winding-down"
    if f.gap_seconds < 30 and f.len > 200: return "focused"
    if f.gap_seconds > 1800 and f.len < 20: return "distracted"
    return "present"

function respond(msg, prev_ts):
    feats = extract_features(msg, prev_ts)
    mode  = classify_mode(feats)
    shape = shape_for(mode)
    audit_log.append({
        "ts":       now_iso(),
        "features": feats,
        "mode":     mode,
        "shape":    shape,
        "user_id":  msg.user_id,
    })
    return agent.reply(msg, response_shape=shape)
# Why this works: composing detection with audit produces the data set
# the team needs to tune thresholds — every classification, every input
# feature, and every chosen shape is logged together, so later you can
# see which inputs produced which modes and adjust the rules.
# Related: see `decision-log`, `embodied-proxy-handoff`.

patterns.intent: Show the salience score, threshold check, and per-window rate limit that gate whether the agent emits at all.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: score every candidate output, emit only over threshold, and enforce a per-window emission cap.
examples.code:
THRESHOLD = 0.65
WINDOW_SECONDS = 1800
MAX_PER_WINDOW = 1

emissions = []          # list of {ts, score}

function salience(event, state):
    return (
        W_NOVELTY      * novelty(event, state.recent)
      + W_GOAL         * goal_relevance(event, state.goals)
      + W_RECENCY      * recency_decay(event.ts)
      + W_PREDERR      * prediction_error(event, state.model)
      - W_FATIGUE      * state.fatigue
    )

function maybe_emit(candidate, state):
    score = salience(candidate, state)
    if score < THRESHOLD:
        log("sub-threshold", candidate, score)
        return
    cutoff = now() - WINDOW_SECONDS
    recent = [e for e in emissions if e.ts >= cutoff]
    if len(recent) >= MAX_PER_WINDOW:
        log("rate-limited", candidate, score)
        return
    emit(candidate)
    emissions.append({"ts": now(), "score": score})
# Invariant: at most MAX_PER_WINDOW emissions per WINDOW_SECONDS; silence is the default.

examples.intent: Production variant: streamed emission of an over-threshold event with backpressure, a cancel-token, and tentative/final markers so the consumer can rollback a push the agent later retracts.
examples.code:
THRESHOLD       = 0.65
WINDOW_SECONDS  = 1800
MAX_PER_WINDOW  = 1
MAX_INFLIGHT    = 1

state = {
    "emissions": load_persisted_emissions(),
    "inflight":  0,
    "cancel":    new_cancel_token(),
}

function over_threshold(candidate):
    return salience(candidate, state) >= THRESHOLD

function window_has_capacity():
    cutoff = now() - WINDOW_SECONDS
    recent = [e for e in state.emissions if e.ts >= cutoff]
    return len(recent) < MAX_PER_WINDOW

function maybe_emit_stream(candidate):
    if not over_threshold(candidate): return
    if state.inflight >= MAX_INFLIGHT: return       # backpressure
    if not window_has_capacity():      return
    state.inflight += 1
    state.cancel    = new_cancel_token()
    try:
        for chunk in render_stream(candidate):
            if state.cancel.fired():
                emit_marker("retracted")
                return
            emit(chunk, marker="tentative")
        # Re-check salience at finalise time — late-arriving evidence
        # can drop the score below threshold while we were streaming.
        if not over_threshold(candidate):
            emit_marker("retracted", reason="score dropped"); return
        emit_marker("final")
        state.emissions.append({"ts": now(), "id": candidate.id})
        persist_emissions(state.emissions)
    finally:
        state.inflight -= 1
# Why this works: scoring once and committing is too eager — the loop
# streams under a cancel token, rechecks salience at finalise, and only
# then counts the emission against the per-window quota, so a stale
# candidate does not consume the budget.
# Related: see `bidirectional-impulse-channel`, `stop-cancel`.

examples.intent: Composition variant: salience-triggered-output composed with `audit`, so every candidate event - emitted, sub-threshold, or rate-limited - lands in a single decision log.
examples.code:
THRESHOLD      = 0.65
WINDOW_SECONDS = 1800
MAX_PER_WINDOW = 1

emissions = []

function salience(event, state):
    return (W_NOVELTY * novelty(event, state.recent)
          + W_GOAL    * goal_relevance(event, state.goals)
          + W_RECENCY * recency_decay(event.ts)
          + W_PREDERR * prediction_error(event, state.model)
          - W_FATIGUE * state.fatigue)

function audit_decision(event, score, decision, extra={}):
    append_audit({
        "ts":       now_iso(),
        "event_id": event.id,
        "score":    score,
        "decision": decision,        # "emit" | "sub-threshold" | "rate-limit"
        "window":   WINDOW_SECONDS,
        "extra":    extra,
    })

function maybe_emit(candidate, state):
    score = salience(candidate, state)
    if score < THRESHOLD:
        audit_decision(candidate, score, "sub-threshold")
        return
    cutoff = now() - WINDOW_SECONDS
    recent = [e for e in emissions if e.ts >= cutoff]
    if len(recent) >= MAX_PER_WINDOW:
        audit_decision(candidate, score, "rate-limit",
                       extra={"recent_count": len(recent)})
        return
    emit(candidate)
    emissions.append({"ts": now(), "score": score, "id": candidate.id})
    audit_decision(candidate, score, "emit")
# Why this works: composing salience with audit produces a complete
# decision log - the team can replay why a given event did or did not
# fire, tune the threshold against ground truth, and detect drift
# when the score distribution shifts under model changes.
# Related: see `decision-log`, `bidirectional-impulse-channel`.

patterns.intent: Show clean interruption of an in-flight agent run: a cancellation token propagated through the loop, the provider stream, and any tool calls, with partial state surfaced to the UI.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: a cancellation token threaded through the agent loop, the provider stream, and tool calls, with partial state returned.
Pass an AbortSignal into streamText; close the stream from the UI, observe the abort event in fullStream, and surface partial output via the onAbort callback.
examples.verified_notes: Confirmed against https://ai-sdk.dev/docs/ai-sdk-core/error-handling: onAbort callback with steps parameter and the 'abort' event in fullStream are both documented verbatim. The abortSignal?: AbortSignal parameter to streamText is documented one hop away at https://ai-sdk.dev/docs/reference/ai-sdk-core/stream-text (linked from the error-handling page); AbortController is the standard Web API that produces an AbortSignal. streamText, openai('gpt-4o-mini'), text-delta and finish events all match the AI SDK Core surface.

examples.intent: Stop in-flight agent speech via session.interrupt(); subscribe to user_interruption_detected for barge-in.
examples.verified_notes: Confirmed against https://docs.livekit.io/agents/build/turns/: the documented Python import is `from livekit.agents import AgentSession, TurnHandlingOptions` (top-level re-export), the `AgentSession(turn_handling=TurnHandlingOptions(...))` constructor, the `@session.on("user_interruption_detected")` and `@session.on("agent_false_interruption")` event handlers, and the `await session.interrupt()` explicit-cancel call are all shown verbatim on the page. The InterruptionOptions fields enabled/mode='adaptive'/resume_false_interruption are documented under the same Turns guide. Fixed the prior `from livekit.agents.voice import TurnHandlingOptions` import (livekit/agents/voice/__init__.py does not re-export TurnHandlingOptions; the canonical import is the top-level livekit.agents one shown in the docs).

patterns.intent: Show a typed event vocabulary streamed to the client (text_delta, tool_start, tool_end, card, done, error) and how the host emits each as it becomes available.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: a typed SSE-style event stream with last-event-id resumption.
Use the Agent's streaming_callback to receive typed chunks (text tokens and tool events) and fan them out as named events.
examples.code:
from haystack.components.agents import Agent
from haystack.components.generators.chat import OpenAIChatGenerator
from haystack.dataclasses import ChatMessage, StreamingChunk


def on_chunk(chunk: StreamingChunk):
    # Haystack hands a StreamingChunk with content plus optional tool events.
    # Translate each into a typed SSE-style event.
    if chunk.content:
        sse_emit(event="text_delta", data={"text": chunk.content})
    for call in getattr(chunk, "tool_calls", []) or []:
        sse_emit(event="tool_start",
                 data={"id": call.id, "name": call.tool_name, "args": call.arguments})
    result = getattr(chunk, "tool_call_result", None)
    if result is not None:
        sse_emit(event="tool_end",
                 data={"id": result.origin.id, "result": result.result})


agent = Agent(
    chat_generator=OpenAIChatGenerator(model="gpt-4o-mini"),
    tools=[...],
    streaming_callback=on_chunk,
)

response = agent.run(messages=[ChatMessage.from_user("Plan my week.")])
sse_emit(event="done", data={})
examples.verified_notes: Verified against https://docs.haystack.deepset.ai/docs/agent and the Haystack data-classes API reference. Confirmed: `from haystack.components.agents import Agent`, `from haystack.components.generators.chat import OpenAIChatGenerator`, `from haystack.dataclasses import ChatMessage, StreamingChunk`, Agent constructor kwargs `chat_generator` / `tools` / `streaming_callback`, `agent.run(messages=...)`, `ChatMessage.from_user(...)`, `StreamingChunk.content`, `StreamingChunk.tool_calls` (list of ToolCallDelta with `.id`, `.tool_name`, `.arguments`), `StreamingChunk.tool_call_result` (singular `ToolCallResult | None` with `.origin` (a `ToolCall` having `.id`) and `.result`). Wave 5 fix: changed `chunk.tool_call_results` iteration to a single `chunk.tool_call_result` null-check to match the documented singular field.

examples.intent: Composition variant: streaming-typed-events composed with `citation-streaming`, so a `citation` event lands as soon as a span is supported, separately from the text deltas it annotates.
examples.code:
# Extended event vocabulary, composed with citation-streaming:
#   text_delta   { text, span_id }
#   citation     { span_id, source, anchor, confidence }
#   tool_start   { id, name, args }
#   tool_end     { id, result }
#   done         {}
#   error        { message }

function handle_request(req, last_event_id=none):
    cursor = resume_cursor(last_event_id) or new_cursor(req)
    cancel = new_cancel_token()
    set_sse_headers()

    for step in agent.run(cursor=cursor, cancel=cancel):
        for chunk in step.stream():
            if cancel.fired(): break
            span_id = chunk.span_id or new_span_id()
            yield sse(id=next_id(), event="text_delta",
                      data={"text": chunk.text, "span_id": span_id})
            # citation-streaming: emit the supporting source AS SOON AS
            # it resolves, in its own typed event, NOT inside text_delta
            for cite in chunk.citations:
                yield sse(id=next_id(), event="citation",
                          data={"span_id":    span_id,
                                "source":     cite.source,
                                "anchor":     cite.anchor,
                                "confidence": cite.confidence})
        for call in step.tool_calls:
            yield sse(id=next_id(), event="tool_start",
                      data={"id": call.id, "name": call.name,
                            "args": call.args})
            yield sse(id=next_id(), event="tool_end",
                      data={"id": call.id, "result": dispatch(call)})

    yield sse(id=next_id(), event="done", data={})
# Why this works: keeping `citation` as a separate typed event lets the
# client render footnote markers and tooltips independently of the
# text deltas they decorate, and lets citations arrive late without
# blocking the visible stream.
# Related: see `citation-streaming`, `structured-output`.

patterns.intent: Show a single Voice interface with speak / listen / converse, a capability-flag check, and a shared event vocabulary so the agent loop swaps providers without code changes.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: an abstract Voice interface with three modes, capability flags, and one event vocabulary across providers.
Production variant: voice loop with backpressure on the mic, a cancel-token wired to barge-in, partial vs final transcript markers, and reconnect-resume keyed on turn id.
Composition variant: unified-voice-interface composed with `streaming-typed-events`, so every provider, mode, and event flows through one typed event vocabulary the client routes on.
examples.code:
# Typed event vocabulary, shared by every Voice implementation and
# every mode (tts | stt | sts) — the client never branches on provider.
#   turn_start | partial_transcript | final_transcript
#   voice_activity_start | voice_activity_stop
#   audio_chunk | barge_in | turn_end | error

function voice_to_typed_stream(voice: Voice, mic):
    last_event_id = 0
    yield sse(id=++last_event_id, event="turn_start",
              data={"capabilities": list(voice.capabilities)})
    if "sts" in voice.capabilities:
        source = voice.converse(mic.stream())
    else:
        source = voice.listen(mic.stream())
    for ev in source:
        # Same vocabulary regardless of which Voice implementation
        # produced the event — that is the unification.
        yield sse(id=++last_event_id, event=ev.type, data=ev.data)
        if ev.type == "turn_end":
            yield sse(id=++last_event_id, event="done", data={})
            return

function client_route(ev):
    switch ev.event:
        case "partial_transcript": ui.show_tentative(ev.data.text)
        case "final_transcript":   ui.commit(ev.data.text)
        case "audio_chunk":        speaker.enqueue(ev.data.audio)
        case "barge_in":           ui.flash("interrupted")
        case "voice_activity_start": ui.mic_indicator(true)
        case "voice_activity_stop":  ui.mic_indicator(false)
        case "error":              ui.error_banner(ev.data.message)
        case "done":               ui.finalise()
# Why this works: the Voice interface unifies the modes, the typed
# event vocabulary unifies the transport, and the two compose so that
# the client gets one switch over event names and the agent loop gets
# one set of providers behind one contract.
# Related: see `streaming-typed-events`, `multilingual-voice-agent`.

patterns.intent: Show the agent emitting declarative interface components over a typed agent-to-frontend protocol, with a thin renderer drawing each and interactions routed back into the loop.

examples.intent: Framework-agnostic skeleton: the agent streams typed UI events (text + declarative components); a generic renderer draws each from an allow-list and posts interactions back.
examples.code:
# Agent-to-UI event vocabulary (declarative; no executable code crosses the wire):
#   text_delta   { text }
#   component    { type, props, id }            # type in renderer allow-list
#   state_patch  { path, value }                # shared state sync
#   interaction  { component_id, event, value } # <- client back to agent
#   done         {}

COMPONENT_REGISTRY = { "form", "chart", "confirm", "choice" }  # renderer allow-list

function run_turn(user_msg, emit):
    for step in agent.run(user_msg):
        for chunk in step.text_stream():
            emit("text_delta", { "text": chunk })
        for ui in step.ui_intents():            # the agent DECIDES the surface
            require(ui.type in COMPONENT_REGISTRY)
            emit("component", { "type": ui.type, "props": ui.props, "id": ui.id })
    emit("done", {})

# Generic renderer (client):
function on_event(ev):
    switch ev.type:
        case "text_delta": append_text(ev.text)
        case "component":  draw(COMPONENT_REGISTRY[ev.type], ev.props, ev.id)
        case "done":       finish()
        default:           drop(ev)             # reject anything off-vocabulary

function on_user_action(component_id, event, value):
    # interaction round-trips back into the agent loop as a new event
    agent.submit("interaction", { "component_id": component_id, "event": event, "value": value })

examples.intent: Generative UI with streamUI: the model answers in text or, per tool call, streams a declarative React component the client renders as it arrives.
Expose a Pydantic AI agent over the AG-UI protocol so any AG-UI frontend renders its declarative components and routes interactions back.

patterns.intent: Pseudo-code skeleton of a voice loop whose turn decision is driven by a semantic completeness score with an acoustic timeout floor, plus a backchannel-versus-barge-in check during playback.

examples.intent: Commit a turn on semantic completeness (or a fallback timeout); ignore backchannels and yield only on real barge-in.
